steps.title: Check for drain hose kinks
steps.text: Unplug the washer. Inspect the drain hose behind the unit and remove any sharp bends that restrict water flow.

steps.title: Clean the drain pump filter
steps.text: Open the access panel, place a towel and shallow tray, then unscrew and clean the drain pump filter of lint and debris.

steps.title: Test the drain pump
steps.text: Reconnect power and run a drain cycle. If you hear humming without water movement, the pump impeller may be blocked or failing.

steps.title: Inspect the door gasket
steps.text: Unplug the washer. Check the rubber door boot for tears, trapped clothing, detergent residue, or debris that prevents the door from sealing.

steps.title: Clean the sealing surface
steps.text: Wipe the door glass and gasket lip with mild cleaner, then remove hair, lint, or coins from the lower gasket fold.

steps.title: Check for overloading
steps.text: Run a small rinse cycle. Overloaded front-load washers can push water past the door seal even when the gasket is not damaged.

guides.title: Washer Fills But Does Not Spin

steps.title: Check load balance
steps.text: Pause the cycle and redistribute heavy items. A severely unbalanced load can stop spin to protect the tub and suspension.

steps.title: Confirm the door lock engages
steps.text: Listen for the door lock click before spin. If the control does not detect a locked door, it will not spin the basket.

steps.title: Inspect belt or motor access
steps.text: Disconnect power before opening panels. Look for a loose drive belt, worn pulley, or motor fault symptoms if the washer hums but the basket does not move.

guides.title: Dryer Takes Too Long to Dry Clothes

steps.title: Clean the lint filter
steps.text: Remove the lint filter and clean it thoroughly before every cycle.

steps.title: Check vent airflow
steps.text: Inspect the vent hose behind the dryer and the exterior vent cap for lint blockages.

steps.title: Test heat output
steps.text: Run a timed dry cycle and verify warm exhaust air is present; weak heat may indicate a failing heater or thermostat.

guides.title: Dryer Runs But Has No Heat

steps.title: Verify airflow first
steps.text: Clean the lint screen and vent path. Restricted airflow can trip safety thermostats and make the dryer stop heating.

steps.title: Check the thermal fuse
steps.text: Disconnect power. Access the blower housing and test the thermal fuse for continuity with a multimeter.

steps.title: Inspect heating element
steps.text: With power disconnected, inspect the heating element coil for breaks or grounding against the heater housing.

guides.title: Dryer Squeals or Thumps While Running

steps.title: Listen for roller noise
steps.text: A rhythmic thump or squeal often comes from worn drum support rollers. Stop the dryer if the drum scrapes or binds.

steps.title: Inspect idler pulley and belt
steps.text: Unplug the dryer before removing panels. Check whether the idler pulley spins freely and whether the belt is cracked or glazed.

steps.title: Check drum glides
steps.text: Look for worn front drum glides or felt seals that let the drum rub against the cabinet.

steps.title: Inspect air damper
steps.text: Check whether the fresh-food air damper is stuck closed and preventing cold airflow from the freezer section.

steps.title: Clean condenser coils
steps.text: Vacuum dust from condenser coils to improve cooling efficiency and reduce compressor load.

steps.title: Confirm evaporator fan operation
steps.text: Listen for the evaporator fan in the freezer compartment; no fan movement can cause poor refrigerator cooling.

steps.title: Clean condenser area
steps.text: Unplug the refrigerator and vacuum the condenser coils and compressor area so the sealed system can reject heat.

steps.title: Listen for start relay clicks
steps.text: Repeated clicking near the compressor can point to a weak start relay, overload, or compressor start problem.

steps.title: Check condenser fan
steps.text: Make sure the condenser fan runs when the compressor runs. A failed fan can cause overheating and poor cooling.

steps.title: Clean the filter
steps.text: Remove the lower rack and clean the dishwasher filter assembly to clear food debris that blocks water circulation.

steps.title: Inspect spray arms
steps.text: Remove or rotate spray arms and clear clogged holes with a toothpick or soft brush.

steps.title: Check water temperature and loading
steps.text: Run hot water at the sink before starting the dishwasher and avoid blocking spray arms with tall dishes.

steps.title: Clear standing water safely
steps.text: Cancel the cycle, disconnect power, and bail out standing water before opening the filter or pump area.

steps.title: Check the drain hose loop
steps.text: Inspect the drain hose under the sink for kinks, clogs, or a missing high loop that lets water flow back.

steps.title: Inspect filter and drain pump
steps.text: Remove the filter cover and check the drain pump inlet for glass, labels, seeds, or other debris.

steps.title: Shut off power and water
steps.text: Stop the cycle, turn off power at the breaker, and close the water supply valve before inspecting underneath.

steps.title: Check hose connections
steps.text: Inspect the inlet hose, drain hose, and clamp connections for loose fittings, cracks, or mineral trails.

steps.title: Inspect pump seal area
steps.text: Look for dripping around the pump and motor assembly. A leaking pump seal usually requires part replacement.

steps.title: Check rack position and airflow
steps.text: Make sure pans are not blocking convection airflow and verify racks are placed according to the cooking mode.

steps.title: Test temperature accuracy
steps.text: Place an oven thermometer in the center of the oven and compare actual temperature against the set temperature after preheat.

steps.title: Inspect bake and broil elements
steps.text: Turn the oven off and let it cool. Look for blistering, breaks, or uneven glowing on the bake and broil elements.

steps.title: Check for gas smell first
steps.text: If you smell gas, stop troubleshooting, leave the area, and contact the gas utility or a qualified technician.

steps.title: Observe igniter glow
steps.text: If there is no gas smell, watch whether the oven igniter glows during bake. A weak igniter may glow but fail to open the gas valve.

steps.title: Inspect burner ports
steps.text: With the oven off and cool, check for food debris blocking burner ports near the igniter.

steps.title: Do not open the cabinet
steps.text: Microwaves can retain dangerous high voltage after unplugging. Do not remove the outer cover or touch internal parts.

steps.title: Check simple external causes
steps.text: Verify the outlet, door closure, timer settings, and turntable operation without opening the appliance.

steps.title: Escalate internal failures
steps.text: No heat, buzzing, burning smells, or arcing usually require a qualified technician because internal high-voltage parts are hazardous.
